# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains qualitative study findings and method details about how experienced professional developers use AI coding agents, including reported motivations, control strategies, task suitability boundaries, sentiments, and study design parameters.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Experienced developers in this study valued AI agents as a productivity boost.

## Source Excerpt

"Through field observations (N=13) and qualitative surveys (N=99), we find that while experienced developers value agents as a productivity boost"

## Note

This statement is reported as a study finding from the authors' observation and survey data.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Experienced developers retained their own agency in software design and implementation when using AI agents.

## Source Excerpt

"they retain their agency in software design and implementation"

## Note

The source presents this as a direct finding from the study population.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: claim-support
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The source attributes developers' retained control to their insistence on fundamental software quality attributes.

## Source Excerpt

"they retain their agency in software design and implementation out of insistence on fundamental software quality attributes"

## Note

This evidence links agent-use behavior to a stated reason, but it does not provide a detailed causal mechanism beyond that reported motivation.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Experienced developers employed control strategies for agent behavior by leveraging their own expertise.

## Source Excerpt

"employing strategies for controlling agent behavior leveraging their expertise"

## Note

The source directly states a how-element of use: developers control agent behavior through expert practice.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper's most salient finding is that professional developers did not vibe code and instead carefully controlled agents through planning and supervision.

## Source Excerpt

"Our most salient finding is that, indeed, professional developers do not vibe code. Instead, they carefully control the agents through planning and supervision."

## Note

This is framed by the authors as their central reported finding.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Experienced developers reported planning before implementing and validating all agentic outputs.

## Source Excerpt

"they plan before implementing and validate all agentic outputs (RQ2)"

## Note

This evidence states specific observed workflow controls rather than a general attitude.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developers found AI agents suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks.

## Source Excerpt

"they find agents suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks"

## Note

This suitability claim is paired in the same finding with an explicit boundary against complex tasks, so the usefulness claim should not be detached from that limitation.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developers found AI agents not suitable for complex tasks.

## Source Excerpt

"they find agents suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks but not complex tasks (RQ3)"

## Note

This is a task-boundary finding. It matters to mechanism only in showing a limit on where agentic coding succeeds, not in explaining the internal cause of failure.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Experienced developers felt overall positive about using agents when they believed they could complement the agents' limitations.

## Source Excerpt

"In addition, experienced developers feel overall positive about incorporating agents into software development given their confidence in complementing the agents’ limitations."

## Note

The positive sentiment is conditional in the source: it is tied to developers' confidence that they can compensate for agent limits.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study combined 13 field observations with a qualitative survey of 99 experienced developers.

## Source Excerpt

"We present a two-part study—13 field observations and a broader survey of 99 experienced developers"

## Note

This evidence describes the study design and sample sizes as presented in the paper.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper defined experienced developers as having at least three years of professional development experience.

## Source Excerpt

"We define experienced developers as those with at least three years of professional development experience."

## Note

This is the source's operational definition for the participant population.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: claim-support
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source cites prior human-study results showing one randomized trial where experienced open source maintainers were slowed by 19% when allowed to use AI, and one issue-tracker deployment where only 8% of invocations resulted in a merged pull request.

## Source Excerpt

"A notable randomized trial found that experienced open source maintainers were actually slowed down by 19% when allowed to use AI ... and an agentic system deployed in an issue tracker saw only 8% of its invocations resulting in complete success (a merged pull request)"

## Note

This is background evidence reported second-hand within the paper rather than generated by the paper's own study, so confidence is lower than for the paper's direct findings. It introduces explicit tension against stronger claims about agentic coding effectiveness.
---

Developers found AI agents suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks.

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---

confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developers found AI agents not suitable for complex tasks.

## Source Excerpt

"they find agents suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks but not complex tasks (RQ3)"

## Note

This is a task-boundary finding. It matters to mechanism only in showing a limit on where agentic coding succeeds, not in explaining the internal cause of failure.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: claim-support
citation: Huang et al., "Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control"
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14012
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source cites prior human-study results showing one randomized trial where experienced open source maintainers were slowed by 19% when allowed to use AI, and one issue-tracker deployment where only 8% of invocations resulted in a merged pull request.

## Source Excerpt

"A notable randomized trial found that experienced open source maintainers were actually slowed down by 19% when allowed to use AI ... and an agentic system deployed in an issue tracker saw only 8% of its invocations resulting in complete success (a merged pull request)"

## Note

This is background evidence reported second-hand within the paper rather than generated by the paper's own study, so confidence is lower than for the paper's direct findings. It introduces explicit tension against stronger claims about agentic coding effectiveness.
```
